sections.content: Overview

Large Language Models have demonstrated remarkable capability in understanding and generating human language, but their raw outputs often contain hallucinations, inconsistencies, and fail to follow complex instructions reliably. This lesson explores the critical techniques that bridge the gap between pre-trained models and production-ready systems: prompt engineering, in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and fine-tuning strategies. Without these techniques, even state-of-the-art models like GPT-4 or Claude produce unreliable outputs for structured tasks, factual accuracy requirements, and domain-specific applications. The problem is fundamental: transformers are trained on vast internet text to predict the next token, but this objective doesn't inherently enforce truthfulness, consistency, or adherence to user instructions. These three core techniques—prompt engineering optimizes the input signal, in-context learning leverages examples to guide behavior, and RAG grounds responses in retrieved documents—form the backbone of practical LLM deployment. Understanding how to layer these techniques determines whether an LLM becomes a production system or remains a research curiosity.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Virat Kohli stepping up to bat in a crucial World Cup semifinal against Pakistan. The bowler (the pre-trained model) possesses raw technical skill—Jasprit Bumrah has perfected his yorker delivery, but the specific line, length, and strategy he employs depend entirely on the captain's instructions and field placement (the prompt engineering). Without clear instructions, Bumrah might bowl defensively or aggressively at random; with precise field settings and hand signals (in-context examples), he adapts perfectly to the situation. The batting coach at the boundary provides real-time feedback—like RAG retrieving relevant match statistics (Kohli's performance against left-arm seamers from previous encounters) to inform tactical decisions. Just as the team's analyst reviews footage of past matches (retrieved context) to brief Kohli on Pakistan's bowling patterns, retrieval-augmented generation feeds the model ground-truth information instead of relying solely on its training data. The field placement (prompt structure), the hand signals (examples), and the tactical brief (retrieved documents) are what transform raw bowling talent into a winning strategy. This reveals why brute-force pre-training alone never achieves reliability: the model needs external direction, examples, and grounded information, just as even the world's best batsman needs a captain's strategy and current match intelligence to play optimally.
Core Concepts
Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering is the art and science of crafting input text that guides a language model toward generating desired outputs with higher quality, accuracy, and relevance. The fundamental insight is that identical models produce dramatically different results when given different prompts—a vague question elicits vague answers, while a structured, role-based prompt with clear constraints generates focused, high-quality responses. Effective prompt engineering employs several established patterns: explicit role assignment ("You are a cricket statistician"), clear task definition, output format specification ("Return JSON with fields: player, runs, wickets"), and constraint statements ("Only use facts from official ICC records"). The reason this works stems from how transformers process text: the prompt establishes semantic context that weights the model's attention mechanisms toward relevant token distributions. Advanced techniques include chain-of-thought prompting, where requesting step-by-step reasoning before the final answer significantly improves accuracy on complex multi-step problems. Few-shot prompting—providing 2-3 correctly-formatted examples before the actual task—also dramatically improves performance by establishing the expected input-output pattern without requiring model retraining. Prompt engineering has become critical because it's the only production-safe way to steer models before fine-tuning, avoiding the cost and complexity of retraining while achieving 20-40% performance improvements on domain tasks.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider MS Dhoni's legendary captaincy in the 2011 Cricket World Cup final against Sri Lanka. Before each ball, Dhoni didn't simply tell Gautam Gambhir "bat now"—instead, he provided precise instructions: "We're chasing 275; the target run rate is 4.5 per over; the field is attacking in the first 15 overs then defensive; here's how Sri Lanka's bowlers performed in similar conditions in the last match." Each instruction (the prompt) shaped Gambhir's batting approach fundamentally. Similarly, telling an LLM "write about cricket" produces generic text, but instructing "You are an ICC statistician. Analyze Rohit Sharma's performance. Return JSON with fields: format, runs, average, centuries. Base analysis only on official scores 2010-2024" produces precise, structured output matching the domain task. The role definition ("You are a statistician"), the format requirement ("Return JSON"), and the constraint ("Official scores only") act like Dhoni's field settings and run-rate targets—they don't change the player's fundamental skill, but they redirect attention and effort toward the desired outcome. Just as Gambhir performed exactly because the prompt (captain's instruction) set clear expectations and constraints, LLMs reliably produce correct outputs when prompted with structured, role-based, format-specific instructions rather than vague requests.
In-Context Learning

In-context learning refers to the remarkable ability of large language models to learn new tasks and patterns from a small number of examples (typically 1-10) provided directly in the prompt, without any weight updates or fine-tuning. This capability emerges from the transformer's attention mechanism: when you include example input-output pairs in the context window before the actual task query, the model's attention layers identify the pattern represented by those examples and apply similar logic to the new query. The mechanism is fundamentally different from traditional machine learning training: the model doesn't update weights, but rather dynamically computes task-specific behaviors from the context alone. Few-shot in-context learning typically outperforms zero-shot (no examples) by 30-50% on structured tasks like classification, entity extraction, and format conversion, without requiring any retraining. The performance improvement comes from several factors: examples reduce ambiguity by demonstrating expected output format, establish semantic patterns that guide token generation, and implicitly constrain the output space toward valid responses. One-shot learning (single example) often provides surprising improvements when the example is well-chosen and representative. The practical advantage is enormous: instead of collecting 10,000 training samples and fine-tuning the model for two weeks, you can add 3-5 examples to your prompt and achieve 70-80% of the fine-tuning performance immediately. This mechanism underlies many production LLM applications where task distribution shifts frequently and retraining is infeasible.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Rohit Sharma being added as a substitute fielder to an unfamiliar team in the final over of a T20 match. He's never played with these teammates, doesn't know their calling signals, and hasn't practiced their field rotations. But the captain quickly shows him two examples: "In the last over, when the batsman faced a yorker, the slip fielder moved to deep cover, and the fine-leg fielder pushed up to short fine leg." By observing just these two examples in-context (the actual situation happening live), Rohit immediately understands the team's fielding philosophy and positioning strategy—he learns the pattern from examples without needing to attend weeks of training camp. His attention (like transformer attention) automatically identifies the common thread: yorker means spread the field toward cover, batsman at the crease means active positioning. He applies this learned pattern to the next delivery without anyone retraining him or updating his muscle memory—he's adapted in real-time from context alone. This is exactly how in-context learning works: the model doesn't update its weights (like Rohit doesn't reprogram his muscle memory), but from the examples provided in the immediate context window, it infers the task structure and applies it correctly to the new input. The power lies in pattern recognition from minimal data—just as Rohit's cricket intelligence lets him learn positioning from two examples, transformer models can infer complex tasks from 2-5 well-chosen examples in the prompt.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) addresses the fundamental limitation of LLMs: they generate outputs based solely on their training data, which becomes stale, incomplete, or incorrect for domain-specific, current, or proprietary information. RAG pipelines augment the model's generation process by first retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base (database, web, enterprise docs), then conditioning the generation on those retrieved documents, effectively grounding the model's outputs in factual, up-to-date information. The architecture consists of three components: a retriever (typically a dense vector embedding model like Sentence-BERT) that searches a knowledge base for relevant chunks when given a query, a ranking module that reorders results by relevance, and finally the LLM generator that produces output conditioned on both the original prompt and the top-k retrieved documents. This approach eliminates hallucination for factual queries—if information doesn't exist in the retrieval corpus, the model cannot fabricate it. Empirically, RAG reduces hallucination rates by 60-80% on factual tasks and improves accuracy from 40-60% baseline (using model knowledge alone) to 85-95% when high-quality documents are retrieved. The retrieval step typically uses semantic similarity: the query is embedded to a high-dimensional vector space, compared against pre-indexed document chunks also embedded in the same space, and the nearest neighbors are returned. RAG scales to knowledge bases with millions of documents because retrieval happens at inference time using efficient approximate nearest-neighbor search (FAISS, Milvus). This technique is critical for production deployments in healthcare, law, finance, and enterprise support where factual accuracy is non-negotiable and models must reference sources rather than hallucinate answers.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the 2019 World Cup semifinal between India and New Zealand. Before the match, India's analytics team didn't rely solely on the captain Virat Kohli's memory of New Zealand's bowling patterns from previous encounters—instead, they retrieved and reviewed precise, current information: official match statistics from the tournament, recent form data on Kane Williamson's captaincy decisions, video footage of Trent Boult's recent bowling spell, and dressing room intelligence from the ground. This retrieved information (the external knowledge base) was then synthesized into the team's batting strategy, feeding specific context to batting order decisions, field placement, and shot selection. Similarly, when asked "What was Jasprit Bumrah's economy rate in T20 World Cups?", an LLM without RAG might hallucinate ("approximately 7.2"), while an LLM with RAG first retrieves the official ICC database containing actual match records, finding Bumrah's true T20 World Cup economy is 6.28, then generates the response grounded in that fact. The retriever (like the analytics team finding relevant statistics) searches a knowledge base (official records) using semantic similarity (matching player name and tournament to the right records), ranks results by relevance (filtering out club-level T20 data), and passes the top results to the LLM (sharing the information with Kohli before batting). The generator doesn't invent an answer—it synthesizes from retrieved facts, just as Kohli's batting strategy isn't invented from memory alone, but informed by current ground intelligence. This reveals why RAG is essential for accuracy: even expert systems (excellent batsmen, powerful models) must ground decisions in current, external facts rather than internal knowledge alone.
Fine-Tuning Strategies

Fine-tuning is the process of updating a pre-trained language model's weights on a task-specific dataset, adapting the model to specialized domains, output formats, or behavioral styles without retraining from scratch. Unlike prompt engineering and in-context learning which work with frozen weights, fine-tuning modifies the model's parameters through gradient descent on labeled task examples. Full fine-tuning (updating all layers) is computationally expensive and risks catastrophic forgetting—erasing pre-trained knowledge—but achieves maximum adaptation to new domains. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and QLoRA address this by updating only a small number of adapter parameters (0.1-1% of total model size) while keeping the base model frozen, reducing memory requirements by 10-20x while achieving 85-95% of full fine-tuning performance. LoRA works by training low-rank decomposition matrices A and B that are applied as residual adapters: instead of updating the full weight matrix W, we train ΔW = AB where A and B are small (rank-4 or rank-8 typical). This is effective because adaptation to specific domains requires comparatively low-dimensional parameter changes—the core linguistic knowledge from pre-training remains intact. Instruction fine-tuning, where models are fine-tuned on diverse task examples with explicit task descriptions, is responsible for the "aligned" behavior of models like ChatGPT and GPT-3.5—these models were fine-tuned on human-written instructions and desired outputs, not pre-trained from scratch. The practical tradeoff: fine-tuning requires labeled data (500-10,000 examples typical), GPU resources (24-48 hours on A100 GPUs), and increases deployment complexity, but provides permanent, task-specific adaptation. When to fine-tune versus using prompt engineering is a production decision: fine-tune for permanent, frequent tasks with sufficient data; use prompting and RAG for ad-hoc tasks, proprietary data, or frequent distribution shifts.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine the Indian cricket team deciding to permanently adapt their batting strategy to face the specific bowling characteristics of Australian pitches at the MCG. They don't just create a one-off instruction to Rohit Sharma before one match—instead, they conduct intensive training camps on Australian ground conditions, have players practice against specific bowling patterns (short-pitched bouncers, seaming deliveries off the Australian grass), and fundamentally refine batting technique through repeated practice on Australian wickets. This is fine-tuning: updating muscle memory (model weights) through repetitive practice (gradient descent) on domain-specific data (Australian match conditions). In contrast, LoRA is like having Rohit focus training on just his footwork against short balls (low-rank adaptation)—not retraining his entire batting style (full fine-tuning), but updating the specific high-leverage technique that matters most for Australian conditions. Without fine-tuning, Rohit could use prompting ("Face the short ball by moving to leg," delivered before each delivery), but this is less reliable than internalized technique. Instruction fine-tuning is analogous to years of Virat Kohli's training under coaches like Greg Chappell—not teaching cricket from scratch, but aligning his natural talent to respond correctly to instructions and guidance. The tradeoff is clear: training camp (fine-tuning) requires weeks away from competition, specific preparation data, and coaching staff—but once complete, the learned technique is permanent and requires no external prompting. This reveals why fine-tuning is powerful but not always necessary: use it when you have labeled domain data and can afford weeks of training; otherwise, prompt engineering and RAG provide faster, more flexible adaptation.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Comprehensive LLM Techniques Demo: Prompt Engineering, In-Context Learning, RAG, and Fine-Tuning
Cricket-themed examples demonstrating all core concepts.
"""

import json
from typing import List, Dict, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

# Simulated LLM response function (in production: OpenAI API, Claude, etc.)
def call_language_model(prompt: str) -> str:
    """
    Simulates LLM inference. In production, this calls OpenAI API, Anthropic, etc.
    """
    # This is a mock—real implementations use actual API calls
    return f"[Generated response based on prompt structure]"

@dataclass
class CricketMatch:
    """Represents a cricket match for context retrieval."""
    match_id: str
    date: str
    team_a: str
    team_b: str
    venue: str
    result: str
    player_stats: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]

@dataclass
class CricketPlayerStat:
    """Individual player statistics."""
    player_name: str
    format: str  # Test, ODI, T20
    runs: int
    wickets: int
    average: float
    economy: float

class CricketKnowledgeBase:
    """
    Simulates a retrieval-augmented generation knowledge base for cricket.
    In production: Pinecone, Milvus, FAISS, or similar vector DB.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        # Sample cricket statistics database
        self.matches: List[CricketMatch] = [
            CricketMatch(
                match_id="WC2019_IND_NZ",
                date="2019-07-10",
                team_a="India",
                team_b="New Zealand",
                venue="Old Trafford",
                result="New Zealand won by 18 runs",
                player_stats={
                    "Virat Kohli": {"runs": 1, "wickets": 0, "format": "ODI"},
                    "Trent Boult": {"runs": 0, "wickets": 2, "format": "ODI"},
                }
            ),
            CricketMatch(
                match_id="T20WC_IND_PAK",
                date="2021-10-24",
                team_a="India",
                team_b="Pakistan",
                venue="Dubai",
                result="India won by 6 wickets",
                player_stats={
                    "Rohit Sharma": {"runs": 74, "wickets": 0, "format": "T20"},
                    "Jasprit Bumrah": {"runs": 0, "wickets": 1, "format": "T20"},
                }
            ),
        ]
        
        self.player_records: Dict[str, CricketPlayerStat] = {
            "Jasprit Bumrah": CricketPlayerStat(
                player_name="Jasprit Bumrah",
                format="T20 World Cup",
                runs=0,
                wickets=22,
                average=0.0,
                economy=6.28
            ),
            "Rohit Sharma": CricketPlayerStat(
                player_name="Rohit Sharma",
                format="ODI",
                runs=17308,
                wickets=0,
                average=48.33,
                economy=0.0
            ),
            "Virat Kohli": CricketPlayerStat(
                player_name="Virat Kohli",
                format="ODI",
                runs=13848,
                wickets=0,
                average=54.52,
                economy=0.0
            ),
        }
    
    def retrieve_player_stats(self
sections.query_format:
str = "ODI") -> CricketPlayerStat:
        """
        RAG retrieval: fetch player statistics from knowledge base.
        In production: use vector similarity search (embedding similarity).
        """
        if player_name in self.player_records:
            return self.player_records[player_name]
        return None
    
    def retrieve_match_info(self
sections.team_b:
str) -> List[CricketMatch]:
        """
        RAG retrieval: find historical matches between two teams.
        """
        results = [
            m for m in self.matches
            if (m.team_a == team_a and m.team_b == team_b) or 
               (m.team_a == team_b and m.team_b == team_a)
        ]
        return results

class PromptEngineer:
    """
    Demonstrates prompt engineering techniques:
    - Role assignment
    - Task specification
    - Output format constraints
    - Chain-of-thought reasoning
    """
    
    @staticmethod
    def zero_shot_prompt(task: str) -> str:
        """
        Zero-shot: No examples, minimal structure.
        Typically produces generic, unreliable outputs.
        """
        prompt = f"""Task: {task}"""
        return prompt
    
    @staticmethod
    def structured_prompt_with_role(
        role: str,
        task: str,
        output_format: str,
        constraints: List[str]
    ) -> str:
        """
        Structured prompt engineering:
        1. Explicit role assignment
        2. Clear task definition
        3. Output format specification
        4. Constraints
        """
        constraints_str = "\n".join([f"- {c}" for c in constraints])
        
        prompt = f"""You are a {role}.

Task: {task}

Output Format:
{output_format}

Constraints:
{constraints_str}

Proceed with the task."""
        return prompt
    
    @staticmethod
    def chain_of_thought_prompt(task: str
sections.analysis_steps:
List[str]) -> str:
        """
        Chain-of-thought: Request step-by-step reasoning before final answer.
        Improves accuracy on complex multi-step problems by 20-40%.
        """
        steps_str = "\n".join([f"{i+1}. {step}" for i, step in enumerate(analysis_steps)])
        
        prompt = f"""Analyze the following task step-by-step:

Task: {task}

Please reason through:
{steps_str}

Then provide your final answer."""
        return prompt

class InContextLearner:
    """
    Demonstrates in-context learning:
    - Few-shot prompting with examples
    - Pattern inference from minimal data
    - Task adaptation without fine-tuning
    """
    
    @staticmethod
    def few_shot_player_analysis(
        examples: List[Dict[str, str]],
        new_player: str
    ) -> str:
        """
        Few-shot in-context learning: Provide 2-3 examples of the desired output format,
        then ask for analysis of a new player.
        The model infers the pattern and applies it to the new input.
        """
        examples_str = ""
        for i, example in enumerate(examples, 1):
            examples_str += f"""
Example {i}:
Player: {example['player']}
Analysis: {example['analysis']}
"""
        
        prompt = f"""Analyze cricket players based on the following pattern:
{examples_str}

Now analyze: {new_player}

Provide analysis in the same format as the examples."""
        return prompt
    
    @staticmethod
    def one_shot_format_conversion(
        format_example: Dict[str, Any],
        task_description: str,
        new_data: Dict[str, Any]
    ) -> str:
        """
        One-shot learning: Even a single well-chosen example significantly improves performance.
        Demonstrates that the model learns task structure from minimal context.
        """
        prompt = f"""Task: {task_description}

Example:
Input: {json.dumps(format_example['input'], indent=2)}
Output: {json.dumps(format_example['output'], indent=2)}

Now convert:
Input: {json.dumps(new_data, indent=2)}
Output:"""
        return prompt

class RetrievalAugmentedGeneration:
    """
    Demonstrates RAG: retrieving external knowledge before generation.
    Reduces hallucination by 60-80% on factual tasks.
    """
    
    def __init__(self
sections.knowledge_base:
CricketKnowledgeBase):
        self.kb = knowledge_base
    
    def rag_query(
        self,
        query: str,
        player_name: str = None,
        team_a: str = None,
        team_b: str = None
    ) -> str:
        """
        RAG Pipeline:
        1. Retrieve relevant documents/facts from knowledge base
        2. Rank by relevance
        3. Condition LLM generation on retrieved context
        """
        retrieved_context = ""
        
        # Retrieve player statistics if querying about a player
        if player_name:
            stats = self.kb.retrieve_player_stats(player_name)
            if stats:
                retrieved_context += f"""
[Retrieved: Player Statistics]
Player: {stats.player_name}
Format: {stats.format}
Runs: {stats.runs}
Wickets: {stats.wickets}
Average: {stats.average}
Economy: {stats.economy}
"""
        
        # Retrieve match history if querying about head-to-head
        if team_a and team_b:
            matches = self.kb.retrieve_match_info(team_a, team_b)
            if matches:
                retrieved_context += f"\n[Retrieved: Match History]\n"
                for match in matches:
                    retrieved_context += f"""
Match: {match.team_a} vs {match.team_b}
Date: {match.date}
Venue: {match.venue}
Result: {match.result}
"""
        
        # Generate response conditioned on retrieved context
        prompt = f"""[System Context]
You are a cricket analyst. Use ONLY the retrieved information below to answer questions.
Do not use information from your training data.

[Retrieved Information]
{retrieved_context}

[User Query]
{query}

[Response]
Based on the retrieved information:"""
        
        return prompt

class FineTuningSimulator:
    """
    Demonstrates fine-tuning concepts:
    - Task-specific training data preparation
    - Parameter-efficient methods (LoRA)
    - Training metrics tracking
    """
    
    @dataclass
    class TrainingExample:
        """A single training example (instruction, output pair)."""
        instruction: str
        output: str
        task_type: str
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.training_data: List[self.TrainingExample] = []
    
    def prepare_instruction_dataset(self) -> List[Dict[str, str]]:
        """
        Prepare instruction fine-tuning dataset.
        Format: task description + desired output.
        Typical: 500-10,000 examples for measurable improvement.
        """
        training_examples = [
            self.TrainingExample(
                instruction="Extract batting statistics for Rohit Sharma from the text.",
                output=json.dumps({
                    "player": "Rohit Sharma",
                    "runs": 17308,
                    "average": 48.33,
                    "format": "ODI"
                }),
                task_type="entity_extraction"
            ),
            self.TrainingExample(
                instruction="Classify the following match outcome as victory or defeat for India.",
                output="Defeat",
                task_type="classification"
            ),
            self.TrainingExample(
                instruction="Summarize Jasprit Bumrah's bowling performance in the last World Cup.",
                output="Bumrah took 22 wickets with an economy of 6.28 in T20 World Cup matches.",
                task_type="summarization"
            ),
        ]
        
        return [{
            "instruction": ex.instruction,
            "output": ex.output,
            "task_type": ex.task_type
        } for ex in training_examples]
    
    def simulate_lora_adaptation(
        self,
        base_model_params: int = 7_000_000_000,
        lora_rank: int = 8
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """
        Simulate LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation):
        - Update only low-rank matrices instead of all parameters
        - Reduces trainable parameters by 100-1000x
        - Achieves 85-95% of full fine-tuning performance
        """
        # LoRA adds trainable matrices of size (hidden_dim, rank)
        # Typical hidden_dim = 4096, rank = 8 gives ~32K trainable params per layer
        # With ~100 adapter layers: ~3.2M trainable params (0.05% of 7B model)
        
        estimated_lora_params = 100 * 4096 * lora_rank  # Approximation
        memory_reduction_factor = base_model_params / estimated_lora_params
        
        return {
            "base_model_parameters": base_model_params,
            "lora_rank": lora_rank,
            "trainable_lora_parameters": estimated_lora_params,
            "parameter_efficiency": f"{(estimated_lora_params / base_model_params * 100):.3f}%",
            "memory_reduction_vs_full_finetune": f"{memory_reduction_factor:.1f}x",
            "expected_performance_recovery": "85-95% of full fine-tuning"
        }

# ============================================================================
# DEMONSTRATION: Integrating all techniques
# ============================================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("LLM TECHNIQUES COMPREHENSIVE DEMO: Cricket Edition")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # 1. PROMPT ENGINEERING
    print("\n1. PROMPT ENGINEERING")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    engineer = PromptEngineer()
    
    # Zero-shot (poor quality)
    zero_shot = engineer.zero_shot_prompt("Analyze Virat Kohli's career.")
    print("Zero-shot prompt (generic, unreliable):")
    print(zero_shot)
    print()
    
    # Structured with role
    structured = engineer.structured_prompt_with_role(
        role="Cricket Statistician",
        task="Extract key career statistics for Virat Kohli",
        output_format="""JSON with fields:
- player_name (string)
- format (string: Test/ODI/T20)
- total_runs (integer)
- average (float)
- centuries (integer)""",
        constraints=[
            "Use only official ICC records",
            "Include only matches where Kohli played",
            "Return valid JSON"
        ]
    )
    print("Structured prompt with role (focused, reliable):")
    print(structured)
    print()
    
    # Chain-of-thought
    cot = engineer.chain_of_thought_prompt(
        task="Why does Rohit Sharma perform better against left-arm seamers?",
        analysis_steps=[
            "Identify Rohit's batting stance and typical movement patterns",
            "Describe how left-arm seamers create problems for right-handers",
            "Explain why Rohit's technique counters this",
            "Cite statistical evidence of his performance against left-armers"
        ]
    )
    print("Chain-of-thought prompt (step-by-step reasoning):")
    print(cot)
    
    # 2. IN-CONTEXT LEARNING
    print("\n2. IN-CONTEXT LEARNING")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    learner = InContextLearner()
    
    # Few-shot prompting
    few_shot_examples = [
        {
            "player": "Jasprit Bumrah",
            "analysis": "Elite bowler with 6.28 economy in T20 World Cup. Strength: death bowling and yorkers. Weakness: early powerplay inconsistency."
        },
        {
            "player": "Pat Cummins",
            "analysis": "All-rounder with balanced contribution. Strength: pace and reliability. Weakness: slower delivery patterns."
        },
    ]
    
    few_shot = learner.few_shot_player_analysis(
        examples=few_shot_examples,
        new_player="Trent Boult"
    )
    print("Few-shot prompting (2 examples guide analysis of new player):")
    print(few_shot)
    print()
    
    # One-shot format conversion
    format_example = {
        "input": {"player": "Rohit Sharma", "runs": 17308, "average": 48.33},
        "output": {"player_id": "RS06", "career_runs": 17308, "efficiency_score": 4.833}
    }
    
    one_shot = learner.one_shot_format_conversion(
        format_example=format_example,
        task_description="Convert cricket statistics to analysis format",
        new_data={"player": "Virat Kohli", "runs": 13848, "average": 54.52}
    )
    print("One-shot prompting (single example establishes pattern):")
    print(one_shot)
    
    # 3. RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION
    print("\n3. RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION (RAG)")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    kb = CricketKnowledgeBase()
    rag = RetrievalAugmentedGeneration(kb)
    
    # RAG query about player statistics
    rag_prompt_1 = rag.rag_query(
        query="What is Jasprit Bumrah's economy rate in T20 World Cups?",
        player_name="Jasprit Bumrah"
    )
    print("RAG Query 1 (player statistics):")
    print(rag_prompt_1)
    print()
    
    # RAG query about head-to-head history
    rag_prompt_2 = rag.rag_query(
        query="What is the head-to-head record between India and Pakistan in recent World Cups?",
        team_a="India",
        team_b="Pakistan"
    )
    print("RAG Query 2 (match history):")
    print(rag_prompt_2)
    
    # 4. FINE-TUNING
    print("\n4. FINE-TUNING STRATEGIES")
    print("-" * 80)
    
    ft = FineTuningSimulator()
    
    # Instruction dataset preparation
    training_data = ft.prepare_instruction_dataset()
    print(f"Prepared {len(training_data)} instruction fine-tuning examples:")
    for i, example in enumerate(training_data, 1):
        print(f"  {i}. [{example['task_type']}] {example['instruction'][:60]}...")
    print()
    
    # LoRA parameters
    lora_stats = ft.simulate_lora_adaptation()
    print("LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) efficiency:")
    for key, value in lora_stats.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("KEY INSIGHTS")
    print("=" * 80)
    print("""
1. PROMPT ENGINEERING: Transforms vague queries into structured tasks.
   Impact: 20-40% quality improvement without model changes.
   
2. IN-CONTEXT LEARNING: Models infer task patterns from 2-5 examples.
   Impact: 30-50% improvement over zero-shot, no retraining needed.
   
3. RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION: Grounds outputs in external knowledge.
   Impact: 60-80% reduction in hallucination, 85-95% accuracy on factual tasks.
   
4. FINE-TUNING: Permanently adapts model to specific tasks.
   Impact: Best accuracy but requires labeled data and GPU resources.
   LoRA: Achieves 85-95% of fine-tuning performance with 100-1000x fewer parameters.
   
PRODUCTION STRATEGY:
- Use PROMPTING + RAG for ad-hoc tasks, proprietary data, or frequent distribution shifts
- Use FINE-TUNING for permanent, high-frequency tasks with sufficient labeled data
- Combine all techniques for production robustness and reliability
    """)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

sections.content: The code demonstrates all four core techniques working together in a cricket context. The `PromptEngineer` class shows progression from zero-shot (generic, unreliable) to structured prompting with role assignment, output format specification, and chain-of-thought reasoning—each step improving reliability by making the task structure explicit. The `InContextLearner` class demonstrates few-shot and one-shot learning: by providing 2-3 examples before the actual task, the model infers the pattern without any weight updates, a remarkable capability that emerges from the transformer's attention mechanism. The `RetrievalAugmentedGeneration` class implements a retrieval pipeline: when queried about player statistics or match history, it first searches a knowledge base (simulating FAISS or Milvus), retrieves relevant facts, ranks them by relevance, then conditions the LLM prompt on those retrieved documents—eliminating hallucination by grounding outputs in factual information. The `FineTuningSimulator` prepares instruction datasets (task-output pairs) for fine-tuning and demonstrates LoRA's efficiency: adapting a 7 billion parameter model by training only 3.2 million parameters (0.05%) while recovering 85-95% of full fine-tuning performance. At runtime, prompt engineering establishes context, in-context examples guide behavior, retrieved facts prevent hallucination, and fine-tuning provides permanent domain adaptation. The code shows that these aren't alternatives—they're complementary techniques applied at different stages of the inference pipeline: use prompting at prompt-time, in-context learning during inference, RAG to augment context, and fine-tuning for permanent adaptation.
How It Works Under the Hood

Understanding the internal mechanics of these techniques reveals why they work and where bottlenecks emerge in production systems. Prompt engineering operates at the attention level: when you structure a prompt with explicit role, task, and format instructions, the transformer's attention layers assign higher weights to tokens matching the specified role and format constraints. Mechanically, the self-attention mechanism computes query-key-value projections for each token, calculates attention scores (softmax of dot products), and uses those scores to route information through the network—a well-structured prompt increases the attention flow toward task-relevant pathways by making the task structure explicit in the token sequence. In-context learning works similarly: example input-output pairs in the prompt create learned attention patterns that the model generalizes to the new query without updating weights. The mechanism appears to involve "in-context parameter binding"—the model temporarily uses examples as context anchors, then maps the new input to the learned pattern. This is why longer context windows (tokens) enable better in-context learning: more examples can be encoded, and the attention mechanism can route more signal through task-relevant pathways. Performance degrades with very long contexts (beyond 2000-4000 tokens) due to quadratic attention complexity—this O(n²) scaling means doubling context length quadruples compute and memory requirements, a critical bottleneck for RAG systems processing long document retrievals. Retrieval-augmented generation introduces a two-stage pipeline: the retriever (typically a dense embedding model) uses approximate nearest-neighbor search to find top-k relevant documents in sub-linear time (using FAISS, LSH, or other indexing), then the full context (query + retrieved documents) is fed to the LLM generator. The retriever is usually a Sentence-BERT or similar encoder fine-tuned for semantic similarity, while the generator remains the original LLM. Bottlenecks here include retrieval latency (must search millions of vectors), ranking quality (false positives pollute context), and context length limits (longer documents reduce capacity for new information). Fine-tuning via gradient descent updates weights across layers to minimize task loss: the optimizer performs backpropagation through the full model (or adapter layers in LoRA), computing gradients and updating parameters. Full fine-tuning requires storing activations for all forward passes, consuming memory proportional to batch size × sequence length × hidden dimension—for a 7B model with batch size 32 and sequence length 2048, this demands 40-80GB GPU memory. LoRA reduces this by training only rank-8 matrices that function as residual adapters; the math: instead of updating W ∈ ℝ^(d_out × d_in), we train ΔW = A × B where A ∈ ℝ^(d_out × r) and B ∈ ℝ^(r × d_in) with rank r ≪ d_out and d_in. This reduces trainable parameters from d_out × d_in to r × (d_out + d_in)—typically 0.1-1% of original—while the low-rank constraint implicitly encodes that domain adaptation requires only a small number of basis directions, a hypothesis born out empirically across tasks.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the mental mechanics of MS Dhoni analyzing a live match moment-by-moment. His pre-trained cricket knowledge (billions of hours watching cricket, executing strategies) is like the LLM's pre-training—vast, multi-faceted, but not yet specialized to the current situation. When Dhoni receives the captain's instructions (prompt engineering: "Field attacking at covers against Rohit's short-ball weakness"), his attention mechanism (brain focus) weights the most relevant information—past footage of Rohit against short balls, current wind direction, Rohit's recent form—more heavily than irrelevant data. By the time the bowler delivers, Dhoni's mental model has routed decision-making through task-relevant pathways (e.g., noticing Rohit's footwork rather than his personality), exactly as transformers route attention to task-relevant tokens. During the match, if Pakistan's coach shows Dhoni a replay of Pakistan's previous World Cup match (in-context examples), he immediately infers the pattern of that bowler's delivery angles without rewatching 100 deliveries—this is in-context learning: temporary adaptation from context alone, no permanent brain rewiring. When Dhoni's team consults real-time statistics on Pakistan's bowling performance in similar ground conditions (RAG: retrieving relevant facts), they don't hallucinate strategy—they ground decisions in factual context, and if the statistics don't mention a particular bowler, the strategy adaptation is constrained accordingly. However, if India plans to permanently dominate Pakistan across seasons, they conduct intensive training camps where players practice specific techniques (fine-tuning), refining muscle memory and neural pathways—this is permanent, gradient-based adaptation requiring investment and repeated practice data. The quadratic attention complexity bottleneck appears in Dhoni's mind when the match has too many concurrent decisions to track (context length limit)—managing 50 simultaneous on-field variables strains focus, while 3-4 key strategic axes are trackable. LoRA is analogous to focusing training on just the most high-leverage skills (say, reading short balls) rather than retraining all cricket techniques, achieving 90% of full training benefit at 10% the time investment. These mechanics reveal why production systems layer all techniques: prompting optimizes token-routing, in-context learning adapts without retraining, RAG prevents hallucination with external facts, and fine-tuning provides permanent domain expertise when data justifies the cost.
